Lxv3W74AAAAJ.keywords: Scientific Machine Learning, Large-scale Optimization, Numerical Methods, Inverse Design in Optics, Metasurfaces, Meta-optics, Topology Optimization, Physics-informed Neural Networks, Deep Neural Operators, PDE Surrogate Models
Lxv3W74AAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Raphael Pestourie is an **Assistant Professor** in the School of Computational Science and Engineering at **Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group focused on the intersection of *computational physics*, *machine learning*, and *large-scale numerical optimization*. His work centers on developing scalable, physics-aware computational methods for <u>inverse design</u> and <u>meta-optics</u>, combining advances in **scientific machine learning** (e.g., neural operators and physics-informed networks), **topology and gradient-based optimization**, and fast approximate solvers to enable practical design of devices that span many wavelengths and millions of degrees of freedom. His homepage (http://www.raphaelpestourie.com/) documents a portfolio of theoretical, algorithmic, and experimental contributions that have had broad impact across optics, photonics, and PDE-based modeling.

---

## Research Areas
Pestourie’s research spans several tightly coupled areas: (1) *inverse design and topology optimization* for large-area metasurfaces and meta-optics, demonstrated in works on optimization of devices with 10^5–10^6 degrees of freedom (e.g., "Topology optimization of freeform large-area metasurfaces" (2019) and "Inverse design of large-area metasurfaces" (2018)); (2) *scientific machine learning* for PDEs, including development of deep surrogate and operator-learning approaches that are both data- and compute-efficient (e.g., "Multifidelity deep neural operators..." (2022), "Physics-enhanced deep surrogates for partial differential equations" (2023)); (3) *physics-informed neural networks and constrained learning* for inverse design, notably methods that enforce hard physical constraints to improve fidelity and reliability ("Physics-informed neural networks with hard constraints for inverse design" (2021)); and (4) *end-to-end co-design of optics and computation* for imaging and sensing, where nanophotonic front-ends are co-optimized with computational back-ends for tasks such as multi-channel imaging and extended depth-of-focus imaging ("End-to-end nanophotonic inverse design for imaging and polarimetry" (2021), "Inverse designed metalenses with extended depth of focus" (2020)). Across these areas he emphasizes scalable algorithms (hybrid solvers, locally periodic approximations, perturbative frameworks) and active/multifidelity learning strategies to make large-scale inverse design tractable and experimentally realizable (e.g., "Active learning of deep surrogates for PDEs" (2020), "HiLAB: A Hybrid Inverse‐Design Framework" (2025)).

---

## Notable Works
- Developed physics-aware learning for constrained inverse design with strong practical impact through the highly-cited framework in "Physics-informed neural networks with hard constraints for inverse design" (2021), which integrates hard physical constraints into neural architectures to improve reliability in engineering inverse problems.  
- Pioneered scalable computational frameworks for designing extremely large metasurfaces in "Inverse design of large-area metasurfaces" (2018) and extended these ideas with topology-optimization techniques in "Topology optimization of freeform large-area metasurfaces" (2019), enabling practical optimization of devices spanning hundreds to thousands of wavelengths.  
- Demonstrated application-driven, large-scale inverse design for high-performance meta-optics—including systems for virtual reality and broadband imaging—in "Inverse design enables large-scale high-performance meta-optics reshaping virtual reality" (2022) and related end-to-end imaging works that co-design optics and algorithms ("End-to-end nanophotonic inverse design for imaging and polarimetry" (2021)).

---

## Academic Background
Pestourie’s publication record indicates a strong interdisciplinary training at the interface of computational science, optics, and applied mathematics. He holds a faculty appointment at Georgia Tech’s School of Computational Science and Engineering and collaborates broadly with academic and industry partners on photonics, PDE modeling, and machine-learning-driven design; his work has accrued over 3,000 citations, reflecting wide adoption of his methods across the photonics and scientific computing communities. His career has combined algorithm development (fast approximate solvers, hybrid inverse-design frameworks), methodological innovation in scientific ML (neural operators, multifidelity learning, physics-enhanced surrogates), and experimental validation of inverse-designed devices. Pestourie’s contributions have been recognized through invited talks, cross-institutional collaborations, and a steady stream of high-impact publications and translational outputs connecting theory to fabricated meta-optics and imaging systems.

Lz6-_iIAAAAJ.keywords: Machine Learning, Natural Language Processing, Artificial Intelligence, Membership inference attacks, Privacy-preserving machine learning, Masked language models, Energy-based models, Controllable text generation, Neural sequence models, Phonological feature representations
Lz6-_iIAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Kartik Goyal is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, affiliated with research groups working on **Machine Learning**, **Natural Language Processing**, and broader **Artificial Intelligence** applications. His work centers on the intersection of principled probabilistic modeling and practical NLP systems, with a particular emphasis on <u>privacy and leakage in language models</u>, <u>structured and interpretable linguistic representations</u>, and <u>decoding and controllable text generation</u>. Across his contributions he blends *theoretical analysis* (e.g., energy-based perspectives and continuous relaxations) with *applied systems* (e.g., resources for phonological representation and tools for low-resource languages), producing methods that are both scientifically grounded and useful for downstream tasks.

---

## Research Areas
Kartik’s research spans several interconnected areas of modern NLP and ML. He has made influential contributions to the study of <u>privacy vulnerabilities of masked language models</u>, developing quantitative membership-inference evaluations that probe how MLMs leak training data (see “Quantifying privacy risks of masked language models using membership inference attacks”, 2022). He also works on <u>energy-based views of language models</u> and sampling/decoding methods (e.g., Metropolis–Hastings analyses and continuous relaxations of decoding like beam search), which underpin a line of work on principled generation and model scoring (“Exposing the Implicit Energy Networks…”, 2021; “A continuous relaxation of beam search…”, 2018). Another major thread is linguistically informed representation: Kartik contributed the widely used **Panphon** resource for mapping IPA segments to articulatory feature vectors, demonstrating that phonological feature vectors can improve downstream learning relative to sparse encodings (2016). He has also advanced methods for *controllable and attribute-guided generation* without expensive fine-tuning (“Mix and match: Learning-free controllable text generation using energy language models”, 2022) and for improved credit assignment in sequence models via differentiable scheduled sampling (2017). Complementing these ML-centric topics, his work includes applications in low-resource and historical text processing—named-entity recognition for Sorani Kurdish and Tajik, typographical analysis of early modern printing, and attribution methods for printers—demonstrating a strong cross-disciplinary interest in both computational linguistics and digital humanities.

## Notable Works
- Quantitative analysis of privacy leakage in masked language models — “Quantifying privacy risks of masked language models using membership inference attacks” (2022). This paper provides rigorous membership-inference evaluations that highlight privacy risks when applying MLMs to sensitive domains (legal, medical).
- Phonological feature resource and representation work — “Panphon: A resource for mapping IPA segments to articulatory feature vectors” (2016). Panphon supplies articulatory feature vectors that have become a practical resource for phonology-aware NLP and multilingual modeling.
- Early metaphor detection using structured kernels — “Identifying metaphorical word use with tree kernels” (2013). An early and cited contribution to computational metaphor detection that combines syntactic structure with kernel methods to improve identification of figurative language.

## Academic Background
Kartik Goyal is based at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he contributes to ML and NLP research and maintains an active publication profile (homepage: https://kartikgo.github.io/). His publication record dates from the early 2010s through the mid-2020s and totals roughly **975 citations**, reflecting sustained impact across methodological and applied topics. His early publications indicate a foundation in computational linguistics and structured prediction (metaphor detection, distributional semantics), while later work demonstrates advanced expertise in probabilistic modeling, decoding algorithms, and privacy analyses for modern language models. Kartik’s portfolio shows collaborations that bridge core ML/NLP labs and cross-disciplinary projects in digital humanities and low-resource language technology, suggesting a researcher who combines rigorous modeling skills with practical, domain-aware applications.
